splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the timing for when actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability from multiemployer pension plans must be finalized. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans, as the timing directly controls how much withdrawing employers must pay.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be selected by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers greater predictability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that a plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the period of court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. The outcome affects revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, extending the court's authority to revoke supervised release beyond the original term.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling the term of supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and courts lack authority to judicially create such a doctrine.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: A longstanding 6-to-3 circuit split exists on whether federal prisoners face a stricter gatekeeping requirement than Congress intended when bringing successive collateral attacks. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners are categorically barred from raising any claim they previously presented, in addition to Section 2255(h)'s requirements. If not, they need only satisfy 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new constitutional law rule.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar on relitigating previously raised claims under § 2244(b)(1) applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, imposing an additional gatekeeping requirement.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s categorical bar does not apply to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions; they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have Article III standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. The outcome determines whether challenges to mail-in voting, ballot counting deadlines, and other election administration practices can be adjudicated before an election or must wait until post-election when the stakes are higher and time is shorter.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate for federal office suffers a cognizable competitive injury sufficient for Article III standing when challenging state election rules that allegedly dilute lawful votes.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's generalized complaint about election rules does not establish a concrete, particularized injury sufficient for Article III standing absent specific evidence of vote dilution.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes priority over agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions.
splits.description: Circuits are divided on whether the rule of lenity trumps deference to agency or Sentencing Commission interpretations of ambiguous criminal provisions. At least three circuits hold lenity prevails, while others (including the Third Circuit panel in Chandler) have held that agency deference trumps lenity. The issue has gained new significance after Loper Bright overruled Chevron deference.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes priority over deference to agency or Sentencing Commission interpretations in ambiguous criminal or sentencing contexts, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through delegated rulemaking.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that deference to the Sentencing Commission's authoritative commentary interpretations takes priority over the rule of lenity when construing ambiguous Guidelines provisions, even in criminal sentencing contexts.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: A deep circuit split exists on whether 'and' in the safety-valve provision means defendants are disqualified only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria or if meeting any single criterion is disqualifying. This directly affects whether district courts must impose sentences without regard to statutory minimums for certain drug offenses and creates geographic disparities in criminal sentencing.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or' based on context and statutory structure.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) includes inchoate offenses like conspiracy, based on Application Note 1's expansion of the guideline text.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which adds conspiracy, aiding and abetting, and attempt to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid authoritative interpretation of the guideline under Stinson/Kisor or impermissibly expands the guideline's text. This affects career-offender designations and significantly impacts sentencing ranges.

positions.label: Commentary validly includes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative and binding interpretation of § 4B1.2(b) under Stinson, and that inchoate offenses like conspiracy qualify as controlled substance offenses for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Commentary impermissibly expands guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline rather than interpreting it, so conspiracy does not qualify as a controlled substance offense.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether non-retroactive changes in law can be considered as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits hold that such changes can never contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons, while others allow courts to consider them. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to resolve this by permitting such consideration, but circuits like the Seventh disagree that the Commission has authority to override their holdings.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law as one factor among others when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons exist for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners must bring challenges to the conditions or execution of their confinement (as opposed to the fact or duration of confinement) exclusively through habeas corpus, or whether they may also pursue civil rights claims.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether challenges to conditions of confinement that do not directly challenge the fact or duration of imprisonment must be brought via habeas or may be brought as civil rights actions. This affects federal prisoners' ability to challenge BOP decisions, including First Step Act time-credit determinations, and has produced irreconcilable lower court decisions for over half a century.

positions.label: Habeas is exclusive remedy for execution-of-sentence claims
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to BOP decisions about the execution of a sentence, including transfer to prerelease custody, must be brought through habeas corpus under 28 U.S.C. § 2241.

positions.label: Civil rights claims also available for conditions challenges
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may bring civil rights claims (e.g., Bivens or § 1983) to challenge conditions of confinement even when those claims relate to the execution of a sentence, because habeas is not the exclusive remedy unless the challenge goes to the fact or duration of confinement itself.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state-controlled substance is not listed on the federal Controlled Substances Act schedules.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires that the substance be federally scheduled, or whether any state-controlled substance qualifies. This affects career-offender and other sentencing enhancements for defendants with prior state drug convictions involving substances not on federal schedules.

positions.label: State schedule controls — no federal-law limitation
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' includes offenses under state law regardless of whether the particular substance is controlled under federal law, as there is no textual basis to graft a federal-law limitation.

positions.label: Federal schedule required
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines only if the substance at issue is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 in good faith under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held it belongs to the estate based on its reading of § 541(a), creating a split with other circuits that protect such appreciation for the debtor under § 348(f)(1)(A).

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion increases in equity inure to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7, based on the broad definition of 'property of the estate' in § 541(a).

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that under § 348(f)(1)(A), post-petition appreciation in property value belongs to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7, consistent with the statute's protective purpose.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may expressly impose punishment for the underlying violation of supervised release conditions at a revocation hearing, or whether revocation sentencing must be limited to the breach of trust in the court's supervisory relationship.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts at supervised release revocation hearings may impose sentences that expressly punish the defendant for the conduct that violated the terms of supervision. The Sixth Circuit's Lewis precedent allows express punishment for violations, placing it at the extreme of a circuit split, with other circuits limiting revocation sentencing to breach-of-trust considerations.

positions.label: Courts may punish for the violation conduct
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly impose punishment for the conduct constituting the violation of supervised release conditions at a revocation hearing.

positions.label: Revocation sentencing limited to breach of trust
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release revocation sentencing should reflect the breach of the court's trust inherent in the violation, not serve as additional punishment for the underlying conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the appeal deadline for a Hyde Amendment attorney's fees order in a criminal case is governed by the civil appeal deadline (30 days) or the criminal appeal deadline (14 days).
splits.description: Circuits have split on whether a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (triggering the 30-day civil appeal deadline) or part of the criminal case (triggering the 14-day criminal appeal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies (30 days)
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Manchester Farming Partnership

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies (14 days)
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is part of the criminal case, so the shorter criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether an order denying a motion to seal medical records qualifies as a collateral order immediately appealable under 28 U.S.C. § 1291. The D.C. Circuit held that maintaining privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of 'high order' warranting immediate review, potentially creating tension with circuits that have taken a narrower view of the collateral order doctrine in sealing contexts.

positions.label: Immediately appealable under collateral order doctrine
positions.summary: Holds that an order denying a motion to seal documents referencing private medical treatments and diagnoses is immediately appealable because medical privacy is a value of 'high order' that would be effectively unreviewable on appeal from final judgment.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal are generally not immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because they can be adequately reviewed on appeal from final judgment or through other means such as mandamus.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination or unfair treatment in the operation of hospitality or business establishments?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to review the Second Circuit's approach to legal standards for discrimination claims in hospitality and service industries. The grant indicates the Court found the Second Circuit's framework sufficiently unsettled or in tension with other circuits' approaches to warrant review, potentially resolving differing standards for fair treatment, discrimination liability, and damages in business establishments.

positions.label: Second Circuit approach
positions.summary: Applied legal standards for evaluating discrimination claims in hospitality establishments that the Supreme Court found sufficiently important or unsettled to review.

positions.label: Competing approach
positions.summary: Other circuits have applied different standards for evaluating discrimination claims in public accommodations and hospitality contexts, creating sufficient disagreement to warrant Supreme Court review.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which prohibits states from enacting laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any motor carrier... with respect to the transportation of property,' extends to preempt state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligently selecting or supervising motor carriers. This affects liability exposure across the trucking industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are 'related to' the broker's services with respect to the transportation of property and are therefore preempted by the FAAAA.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent hiring or supervision of carriers are not preempted because they do not relate to the broker's 'prices, routes, or services.'

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on the scope of FIFRA's preemption of state failure-to-warn claims. The question is whether FIFRA's provision that states cannot impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal requirements bars state tort claims that effectively require additional label warnings the EPA has not mandated.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling changes beyond what the EPA has mandated, because such claims impose requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal labeling requirements.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims survive FIFRA preemption when they are parallel to or consistent with FIFRA's misbranding provisions, because FIFRA does not occupy the entire field of pesticide labeling claims.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that overlap with habeas corpus grounds — such as alleged errors or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence — may be considered in compassionate release motions. Some circuits allow such considerations as one factor, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used as a substitute for § 2255 habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider claimed legal errors or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot use compassionate release for habeas-type claims
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a vehicle for relitigating the validity of a conviction or sentence, and such claims must be brought under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
